title: How to Verify Income Without Tax Returns: 5 Methods Lenders Use
meta: Many borrowers can't qualify using tax returns. Here are 5 methods lenders use to verify income without tax returns — from bank statements to asset depletion to P&L loans.
content:
Why Income Verification Without Tax Returns Matters

Traditional mortgage and loan underwriting relies heavily on tax returns to verify income. But for a growing segment of borrowers — self-employed individuals, business owners, real estate investors, and gig workers — tax returns dramatically understate actual earning capacity because of legitimate deductions, depreciation, and pass-through losses.

In 2026, lenders have five established methods to verify income without relying on tax returns. Each suits different borrower profiles.

Method 1: Bank Statement Analysis

The most widely used alternative income verification method. Lenders review 12–24 months of bank statements to calculate average monthly deposits as a proxy for income.

Best for: Self-employed borrowers with strong cash flow, business owners who write off significant expenses

How it works: Total qualifying deposits over the statement period, exclude non-income items, divide by months, apply an expense factor (for business accounts). AI tools like StatementScrub automate this calculation in under 30 seconds.

Loan types: Non-QM mortgages, business loans, MCA, personal loans

Method 2: Profit and Loss Statement

A CPA-prepared or accountant-signed P&L statement covering the most recent 12–24 months. Some lenders accept P&L-only qualification without bank statements or tax returns.

Best for: Business owners with clean books and an accountant relationship; newer businesses that don't yet have 2 years of tax history

Caution: P&L-only loans have higher fraud risk; most lenders require the P&L to be CPA-prepared and may cross-reference with bank deposits

Method 3: Asset Depletion / Asset Dissipation

For borrowers with significant liquid assets but limited income, lenders calculate a synthetic monthly income by dividing liquid assets by the loan term in months.

Example: $900,000 in liquid assets ÷ 360 months (30-year loan) = $2,500/month qualifying income

Best for: Retired borrowers, high-net-worth individuals with investment portfolios, borrowers who recently sold a business

Eligible assets typically: Checking/savings, money market accounts, stocks, bonds, retirement accounts (with haircut)

Method 4: Rental Income from Lease Agreements

For real estate investors, rental income from executed lease agreements can be used as qualifying income — sometimes even without requiring 2 years of rental history.

How it works: 75% of gross rental income from executed leases is counted as qualifying income (25% vacancy/expense factor)

Best for: Real estate investors, landlords qualifying for new rental property purchases

Related: Rental income verification guide

Method 5: 1099 Income Verification

Freelancers, consultants, and independent contractors who receive 1099 forms can use these as income documentation. Unlike W-2 employees, 1099 income typically requires a 2-year history and the lender will average the last two years of 1099 income.

Best for: Consistent freelancers and consultants with steady 1099 clients

Limitation: Variable year-to-year 1099 income creates challenges — lenders will average or use the lower of two years

Comparing the Methods

MethodTax Returns Required?Best Rate Tier?
Bank statement loanNoNear-conventional
P&L statementNoHigher premium
Asset depletionNoVaries by LTV
Rental incomeSometimesStandard investment
1099 incomeSometimes (2 years 1099)Near-conventional

Related: Bank statement loan programs in 2026 | Verifying self-employed income for a mortgage | Self-employed income verification

Bottom Line

Tax returns are one income verification method — not the only one. For self-employed borrowers and business owners, bank statement analysis typically delivers the strongest qualifying income while maintaining relatively competitive rates. Understanding all five methods helps both borrowers and brokers find the right documentation strategy for each situation.
